core_intuitions: The latency win is a reduction in SERIAL DEPTH, not FLOPs: AR needs L sequential forward passes (one token per pass) while masked diffusion needs only N ≪ L parallel denoising steps that each process the whole sequence at once.

The forward process is absorbing masking (clean → progressively [MASK] → all [MASK]) with α_t = probability a token survives; the reverse-process training loss collapses to a mask-rate-weighted cross-entropy computed ONLY on masked positions — a continuous-time, order-agnostic generalization of BERT-style masked LM.

A single denoising step predicts all masked tokens CONDITIONALLY INDEPENDENTLY, which is wrong for language; iterative confidence-based remasking (commit the confident few, re-condition, repeat) recovers the inter-token correlations — more steps trade compute for coherence.

Block diffusion interpolates AR and diffusion: generate blocks left-to-right (block-causal mask, KV-cache reuse) but denoise the tokens WITHIN a block in parallel (bidirectional); block size B is the dial from pure AR (B=1) to pure diffusion (B=L), and enables flexible length.

Bidirectionality is a separate structural benefit from speed: every position conditions on every other, giving native infilling (clamp prefix AND suffix, denoise the hole) and robustness to the reversal curse.
The economic trade: diffusion wins single-stream latency at low-to-moderate batch, but does more total compute per token, so batched AR throughput catches up at high concurrency.

new_figures.name: dllm-serial-depth-ar-vs-diffusion
new_figures.concept: Why diffusion is faster — serial depth L (AR, one token per pass) versus N ≪ L (diffusion, whole row per pass), at equal output length.
new_figures.rationale: This is the load-bearing intuition of the whole chapter: the speedup is a reduction in the number of DEPENDENT steps, not total work. Watching AR crawl one cell per pass while diffusion resolves the entire row in a few passes makes 'serial depth' concrete in a way the T_AR ≈ L·t and T_NAR ≈ N·t' equations cannot.
new_figures.brief: Two horizontal lanes sharing a token axis of 8 illustrative positions (small integer, no benchmark numbers). TOP lane 'Autoregressive — one token per forward pass': animate step-by-step, step 1 emits token 1 (a solid cell appears, fed by a small 'forward pass' box on the left), step 2 emits token 2 conditioned on the growing left context (draw a thin left-to-right context arrow and a small growing 'KV cache' chip), … 8 sequential steps to fill 8 tokens; a 'serial depth' counter climbs 1→2→…8. BOTTOM lane 'Masked diffusion — all positions per forward pass': start with all 8 cells as [MASK] (--muted); animate ~3 sweeps (N=3), each sweep is ONE forward pass over the whole row that resolves several masks at once (light up multiple cells per frame, --accent); a 'serial depth' counter reaches 3. Make the diffusion 'forward pass' box visibly fatter/heavier than the AR one and tag it 'compute-heavier per step' so the FLOPs-not-free caveat is pre-empted. Static end-frame must read clearly: AR lane 'serial depth = L = 8 passes', diffusion lane 'serial depth = N = 3 passes, N ≪ L', plus a caption strip 'same output length; fewer DEPENDENT steps → lower latency. Latency ∝ serial depth.' Counters in --good, AR tokens --ink, masks --muted, resolved diffusion tokens --accent. Everything inside the viewBox; clear top-to-bottom reading order; include the ↻ replay button per repo convention.

new_figures.name: dllm-masked-objective
new_figures.concept: The absorbing forward (mask) process and the masked-only, mask-rate-weighted cross-entropy training objective.
new_figures.rationale: The Discrete & Masked Diffusion section is pure math (the forward q(x_t|x_0), the α_t schedule, and the weighted loss). A single infographic that shows tokens actually being masked as t grows, and pins the loss to ONLY the masked cells with the 1/(1-α_t) weight, turns three stacked equations into one glance and cements why this is 'BERT at every masking rate, weighted correctly.'
new_figures.placement: The reverse (denoising) process and the objective
new_figures.brief: One short illustrative sentence of 6 tokens (e.g. 'the cat sat on the mat'). Draw the forward corruption as three rows top-to-bottom at increasing time t: row t=0 all 6 tokens clean; row t (middle) with 2 of 6 replaced by [MASK]; row t=1 all 6 are [MASK]. In the left margin annotate the schedule with a small monotone curve dropping from 1 to 0: 'α_t = P(token survives unmasked), α_0=1, α_1=0'. From the MIDDLE (partially-masked) row, draw an upward arrow into a box 'bidirectional transformer — no causal mask' that reads the whole row and outputs predicted clean tokens; show predictions emerging ONLY at the masked positions. Below the middle row, tag each cell: masked cells get a small --accent 'loss here' tag, unmasked cells get a grey 'context — no loss' tag, making 'only masked positions contribute' unmistakable. Present the objective compactly near the transformer box: L = [1/(1-α_t)] · Σ over masked i of −log p(x0^i | x_t), with the weight boxed and two annotations: 't→0: few masks → weight large, each rare mask counts heavily' and 't→1: many masks → reconstruct from almost nothing.' Add a small caption 'no left-to-right order: any subset recovered from any context.' Theme-safe: clean tokens --ink, masks --muted, loss tags --accent, weight/annotation text --ink-soft. Single static frame, all inside viewBox, clear reading order top(t=0)→bottom(t=1) then up into the model.

new_figures.name: dllm-confidence-remasking-trajectory
new_figures.concept: Iterative parallel denoising with confidence-based remasking, and why iterating beats filling everything in one step (the conditional-independence trap).
new_figures.rationale: This is the sampler at the heart of the chapter and the answer to the Interview Corner question. The commit-the-confident / remask-the-rest / re-condition loop is a genuine multi-step process that a static frame cannot fully convey — you have to SEE a committed token become context that sharpens the next step's predictions. It also visually resolves the conditional-independence pitfall.
new_figures.placement: Iterative Parallel Denoising: A Tiny Sampler
new_figures.brief: One sequence of 8 cells, all starting as [MASK] (--muted). Animate ~4 denoising steps. EACH step: (1) the bidirectional pass predicts ALL currently-masked positions in parallel — render a faint tentative token in each masked cell, each with a small confidence bar or opacity shade; (2) COMMIT only the top-few most confident predictions — they become solid --accent with a small lock glyph; (3) the remaining low-confidence cells return to [MASK]. On the NEXT step, draw thin bidirectional context arrows from the committed cells into the still-masked cells and show the new tentative predictions arriving with visibly higher confidence (fuller bars) — this is the re-conditioning 'aha'. A step counter (1..4) and a shrinking masked-count gauge following a roughly linear schedule (8 → ~5 → ~2 → 0). Include a compact side panel contrasting ONE-SHOT vs ITERATE: 'one-shot: fill all 8 at once → each token individually plausible but jointly incoherent' with a tiny crossed-out example of subject/verb disagreement, versus 'iterate: commit confident, re-condition → coherent.' Static end-frame must read clearly: fully resolved sentence + caption 'each committed token becomes context for the next step; N steps recover the correlations one step throws away — more steps trade compute for coherence.' Theme-safe: masks --muted, committed tokens --accent, confidence via opacity/height, arrows --ink-soft. All inside viewBox; include the ↻ replay button.

new_figures.name: dllm-block-diffusion-structure
new_figures.concept: Semi-autoregressive block diffusion: autoregressive across blocks (block-causal, KV-cached) plus parallel diffusion within each block (bidirectional).
new_figures.rationale: Block diffusion is the production architecture that maps onto existing AR serving stacks, and its power is in the attention structure — bidirectional within a block, causal across blocks — which is exactly what enables KV-cache reuse and flexible length. A single composed frame showing finalized-and-cached earlier blocks, a mid-denoise current block, and the block-causal mask inset makes the hybrid click far faster than the prose + ASCII sketch.
new_figures.brief: A left-to-right chain of 3 blocks, 4 tokens each (small illustrative integers). Blocks 1 and 2 are FINALIZED: solid tokens (--ink/--accent), each with a 'KV cache' chip beneath ('keys/values fixed — computed once, reused'). Block 3 is the CURRENT block, shown mid-denoise: 2 tokens committed (--accent) and 2 still [MASK] (--muted), with a small badge 'parallel denoise, N_B steps' and a curved bidirectional double-arrow INSIDE the block indicating every in-block position sees every other. Across blocks draw a causal arrow chain block1→block2→block3 labeled 'autoregressive across blocks (previous blocks finalized).' Include a small L×L attention-mask inset (12×12 for 3 blocks of 4): dense filled squares on the diagonal blocks (bidirectional within block), filled rectangles below the diagonal (a block attends to all earlier blocks), and empty cells above (a block cannot see future blocks) — filled = --v-fill/--accent, blocked = empty with --v-grid outline; label it 'block-causal mask.' Bottom dial annotation: 'B = 1 → pure autoregressive  •  B = L → pure diffusion.' Two side tags: 'flexible length: keep emitting blocks until [EOS]' and 'cache reuse: only the current block is recomputed.' Theme-safe, single static frame, everything inside viewBox with clear left-to-right reading order and the mask inset clearly separated.

new_figures.name: dllm-bidirectional-infilling
new_figures.concept: The bidirectional advantage — native infilling (denoise a hole conditioned on both sides) and robustness to the reversal curse.
new_figures.rationale: Bidirectionality is the chapter's strongest argument that non-AR LMs are a different tool, not a faster clone. Contrasting two-sided infilling against AR's left-only view, and showing why 'A is B' training leaves AR stuck on 'what is B?' while a bidirectional objective is not directionally biased, is more memorable as a picture than in prose. Nice-to-have because the text and code example already cover it well.
new_figures.placement: The Bidirectional Advantage: Infilling, Constraints & the Reversal Curse
new_figures.brief: Two stacked panels. TOP 'Infilling': a row with a clamped prefix (2 solid tokens, left) and a clamped suffix (2 solid tokens, right) and a masked HOLE (2 [MASK] cells) in the middle; draw arrows converging into the hole from BOTH the prefix and the suffix, labeled 'conditioned on both sides.' Directly beneath, a small AR strip of the same sentence with arrows flowing ONLY left-to-right into the hole, labeled 'AR: the future to the right is unavailable — must bolt on fill-in-the-middle.' BOTTOM 'Reversal curse': show a training pair 'A is B'; for AR draw a single gradient arrow A→B and a query 'what is B?' leading to a grey '?' (stumbles); for diffusion draw bidirectional reconstruction arrows A↔B (either side masked during training) and the same query 'what is B?' resolving to 'A' (--good check). Keep the reversal tokens abstract (A, B) and the infill sentence short and illustrative. Theme-safe: clamped/known tokens --ink/--accent, masks --muted, failure '?' --muted, success --good. Single static frame, both panels inside viewBox with clear top-to-bottom reading order.

summary: This dense, concept-rich chapter currently has ZERO figures, so the plan proposes a disciplined foundational set of five, one per major mechanism. Two long-animations carry the ideas that genuinely need motion: dllm-serial-depth-ar-vs-diffusion (AR crawling one token per pass vs diffusion resolving the whole row in N ≪ L sweeps — the chapter's central serial-depth intuition) and dllm-confidence-remasking-trajectory (the commit-confident / remask / re-condition sampler loop, which also visually resolves the conditional-independence trap). Three statics carry the rest: dllm-masked-objective (forward masking + the masked-only weighted cross-entropy), dllm-block-diffusion-structure (AR-across-blocks + diffusion-within-block with the block-causal mask and KV-cache reuse), and dllm-bidirectional-infilling (two-sided infilling and reversal-curse robustness, priority 3). All are theme-safe, use only small illustrative integers, and are legible as static frames.
